schools.summary: Henry reads 'the way of all the earth' as a passage to a better life and lays out David's charge as rule, spirit, and reasons.
Keil: 'be a man' is not stoic composure but bravery to keep God's commandments; the idiom echoes the Torah's 'keep the keeping of Jehovah.'
Gill grounds David's numbered days in providence and cites Abarbinel that he is named 'David,' not 'King David,' since a man has no power in death.

themes: the way of all the earth
keep the charge of the Lord

schools.summary: Clarke turns the verse into an elegy on death the leveller, then reads 'show thyself a man' as acting rationally and being devoted to God.
Gill: death is a decree that can never be reversed, borrowed by David from Joshua's own farewell.
Keil binds 'be a man' to keeping the commandments; Ellicott notes the echo of Joshua 23:14.

themes: the throne no defence against the grave

schools.summary: Clarke distinguishes the five terms and argues good is commanded because it is inherently good, not good merely because commanded.
Ellicott judges the religious charge noble, chiming with David's last words and the prayer of 1 Chronicles 29 — but warns the special charges show worldly prudence.
Gill takes 'the charge' to cover both David's whole walk before God and his just government of Israel.

outliers.position: A thing is not good because God commands it; God commands the good because it is inherently good and useful.

themes: keep the charge of the Lord
the Law of Moses as rule

schools.summary: Keil traces the word to 2 Samuel 7 and shows the establishing of the throne is grammatically subordinate to Solomon's own obedient walking.
Henry distinguishes the absolute Messianic promise from the conditional promise of a man on the throne — 'we never let fall the promise till we let fall the precept.'
Clarke: the sons did not stay faithful, and 'God's justice cut off the entail made by his mercy.'
Gill: the word is God's promise concerning David's kingdom and its succession, confirmed by obedience.
Keil reads 'according to thy wisdom' as marking the safe opportunity to punish so powerful a general; Ellicott grants the charge is within the letter of the law but sees a rankling grudge behind it.
Gill widens Joab's guilt to Absalom's death, his insolence, and his late treason with Adonijah.
Henry: the charge sprang not from personal anger but from concern for the security of Solomon's throne.
Clarke dwells on the treachery — Joab stabbed Abner and Amasa while feigning an embrace.

debates.question: Is David's charge against Joab righteous justice or personal grudge?

sides.position: Justice for the security of the throne, within the letter of the law
Largely just, yet shadowed by long-remembered grudge

themes: the blood of Abner and Amasa
blood of war shed in peace

schools.summary: Clarke, elsewhere Solomon's critic, holds it would have insulted justice to spare Joab; David could not have died in God's approbation without commanding it.
Keil: 'in peace' means unpunished; executing so powerful a general required wisdom to forestall an army revolt.
Gill: Solomon is to use his wisdom to take the fittest opportunity to cut off a man dangerous to the peace of his government.

Keil balances gratitude against justice and presses that 'eating at thy table' means joining the king's own meals, not merely drawing court rations; Ellicott traces the kindness to Chimham's inheritance near Bethlehem.
Henry: kindnesses received must not be buried in the grave; children must repay their parents' friends — a rule he sees echoed in Proverbs 27:10.
Gill: Barzillai being now dead, the kindness is to fall on his posterity for his sake.

Keil justifies the charge — David forgave the private wrong but could not remit the public insult to the Lord's anointed; Ellicott calls it the most ungenerous charge, betraying an old grudge though partly dictated by policy.
Gill reads Shimei as a hidden enemy, now on Solomon's side but not to be trusted.

debates.question: Is the charge against Shimei righteous or a vindictive breach of David's oath?

sides.position: A just guarding of the anointed office and the throne
An ungenerous withdrawal of a free pardon, betraying an old grudge

schools.summary: Clarke, with Kennicott, argues the unexpressed negative carries over — 'bring not down his hoary head' — so David commands restraint, not death, consistent with Solomon later only confining Shimei.
Keil: Solomon the wise man knows what to do; the words are a guarded sentence of justice.
Gill: David's oath bound only himself; Solomon need not acquit a fresh offender.

debates.question: Does the verse command Shimei's death or forbid it?

sides.position: It licenses Solomon to punish a proven offender without breaking David's personal oath
Rightly pointed, the carried-over negative forbids shedding his blood — David commands restraint

schools.summary: Keil and Ellicott fix the burial on Mount Zion, the regular royal tombs, still standing in Peter's day (Acts 2:29); Clarke doubts Josephus's tale of buried treasure.
Henry: David served his generation and saw corruption (Acts 13:36); his epitaph is 'the last words of David,' closing in the hope of resurrection.
Gill: 'slept with his fathers' means he died as they died, not that he shared their grave, and dates the death to about Pentecost.

themes: buried in the city of David
pointing to the Son of David

schools.summary: Keil reads vv.11-12 as a heading expanded in 1 Chronicles 29, framing the chapter's second half as Solomon establishing his throne by justice on the rebels and by his father's charge.
Gill confirms the forty years from the pagan historian Eupolemus and notes the odd months are dropped to leave the even sum.
Henry rejoices that one good reign passed peaceably into another, but reads 'established greatly' as anticipating the necessary removal of Adonijah, who ruined himself by presuming on patience.
Ellicott sees disaffection lurking beneath the smooth surface, with rebellion abroad (1 Kings 11:14-25) crushed so that Solomon's royalty rises to colossal greatness.
Gill: Solomon already sat by David's appointment and now held the throne by the whole people's agreement.

Keil hears wariness in 'Is peace thy coming?' recalling Adonijah's earlier bid (1 Kings 1:5); Ellicott explains the queen-mother's great dignity in Eastern courts, which makes Bathsheba the ideal, disarming advocate.
Gill, with Abarbinel, dates the visit to a few days after David's death.
Keil frames the dialogue: Adonijah secures Bathsheba's attention and goodwill before naming grievance and petition, a courtesy that runs all the way to Solomon's reverent reception of his mother.
Gill: 'I have something to say' signals that Adonijah came upon business, not a social call.

Ellicott hears craft flattering Bathsheba and exposes the covert design — by Eastern custom taking a king's wife was the successor's claim (Ahithophel's counsel, 2 Samuel 16:21); Keil grants the birthright was really his.
Clarke ranks the threefold human title — primogeniture, the people's voice, the high priest's consent — under the paramount right of God, who chose Solomon.
Gill allows the crown was Adonijah's by birthright as the eldest son.

themes: it was his from the Lord
the successor's claim to a king's wife

schools.summary: Keil shows the 'small' petition is the snare — framed in humility and reduced to one item to slip past a mother's guard, though Solomon will read it as a reach for the throne.
Gill notes the deliberate diminishment: 'one petition... and a small one too,' as Bathsheba herself will call it.

themes: a snare for a mother's guard
the boldest move in the meekest words

schools.summary: Clarke takes the request as innocent and denounces reading it as treason — a flimsy pretext to shed a brother's blood; whoever varnishes Solomon by state necessity or divine command is an enemy to truth.
Keil and Ellicott read the suit as a veiled political bid, since by custom a king's wife was the successor's prize.
Gill sees a 'design' that Adonijah flatteringly seeks to cover by owning Solomon as king.

debates.question: Was Adonijah's request for Abishag an innocent suit or a renewed grasp at the crown?

sides.position: Innocent desire for a wife; the execution was a pretext for judicial murder
A veiled political bid Solomon rightly discerned as treason

schools.summary: Henry marvels that Bathsheba suspected neither the treason nor the incest, calls it her weakness and folly, and warns that those with a prince's ear must never lend it to further a wicked design.
Gill catches her unsuspecting goodwill: 'Very well spoken, the thing is good and right.'

Henry reads Solomon's rising, bowing, and seating his mother at his right hand as the fifth commandment kept — greatness does not cancel a child's honour to a parent — yet the promise 'not to say her nay' held a limit: only what is just.
Keil and Ellicott mark the reverence due the queen-mother, a rank of great dignity in Eastern courts, which raises the stakes of her coming petition.
Gill notes she came from her own apartment to present her suit.

themes: the queen-mother at the right hand

schools.summary: Keil renders Solomon's warm assent turning to indignation the moment Abishag is named — 'ask for him the kingdom' — naming Abiathar and Joab as backers of the plot, and defends the vehement text against Thenius's emendations.
Gill hears the diminishing note: one little petition, which Bathsheba hoped would be granted.
Keil lets the request run into Solomon's retort — 'ask for him the kingdom also' — where 'brother' becomes the ground of alarm, and the king swears the suit is spoken against Adonijah's own life.
Gill notes Bathsheba leans on kinship — 'thy brother' — to move the king to grant it.

Ellicott hears bitterness venting in irony, like his father's bursts of passion over Nabal, and conjectures Abishag was the 'fair Shulamite' Solomon already loved; the king executes vengeance summarily, without trial.
Gill reads Solomon as courteous to his mother but 'inwardly fired,' unable to forbear tartness and resentment at the request.

Ellicott traces the imprecation formula across the histories and suggests it was accompanied by a gesture signifying destruction; Keil takes the oath in earnest — Adonijah has spoken against his own life.
Gill sees the oath as closing the matter to prevent his mother pressing the petition further.

themes: a throne suffering no second rising

schools.summary: Keil holds the sentence perfectly just — duty to God demanded the twice-plotting rebel be punished without regard to kinship; Ellicott reads the adjuration as showing Solomon the king, his personal self merged in his royalty, unlike David the living man.
Gill perceives from the second oath how warm, earnest, and resolute Solomon was against the petition.
Clarke protests that Adonijah was suspected, condemned, and slain without a hearing, and denounces Calmet's maxim that policy may override the counsels of the Gospel as diabolic.
Keil holds the execution perfectly just on a twice-plotting rebel; Ellicott notes the body-guard's chief served as executioner, as in the days of the judges.
Gill notes only the agents — Benaiah, perhaps with the Cherethites and Pelethites.

debates.question: Was the summary execution of Adonijah just, or judicial murder without a hearing?

sides.position: Righteous justice on a persistent rebel, demanded by duty to God
A brother slain on suspicion without trial; no plea of state necessity excuses it

schools.summary: Keil reads the sparing of Abiathar as proof Solomon acted without revenge; Ellicott stresses it was not his priesthood but his long friendship to David that protected him, and locates Anathoth as Jeremiah's birthplace.
Henry contrasts Saul's slaughter of Abiathar's house with Solomon's sparing of a really guilty man, drawing the rule that kindness to God's people is remembered to their advantage.

Keil and Ellicott read the deposition as fulfilling the word against Eli's house (1 Samuel 2-3); Ellicott insists the words describe the effect, not the purpose — Ithamar degraded, Eleazar restored in Zadok.
Clarke traces the priesthood reverting from Ithamar's last heir Abiathar to Eleazar's line in Zadok, a deserved degradation for backing Adonijah.
Gill stresses the deposition was Solomon's royal act, not requiring a college of priests, against Roman controversialists.

Keil fixes the altar on Zion and defends the text that Joab followed Adonijah not Absalom; Ellicott explains Solomon charges only the old murders so as to bring Joab under the Law that no sanctuary shields a wilful murderer (Exodus 21:14; Numbers 35:33).
Clarke reads Joab's flight to the altar as claiming divine protection, or perhaps a spark of remorse wishing to die in the house of God.
Gill supplies the trigger — tidings of Adonijah's death and Abiathar's deposition.

themes: flight to the horns of the altar

schools.summary: Keil links Solomon's order to the demands of blood-guilt — Joab dies that the innocent blood of Abner and Amasa be taken from David's house — a just sentence carried out even at the altar.
Gill pictures the report reaching the king by his courtiers, sending Benaiah the executioner.

Clarke explains the ancient right of sanctuary and its abuse, and the Law's exception that a presumptuous murderer be dragged from the altar (Exodus 21:14) — a principle he shows even the pagan Euripides acknowledged.
Gill locates the scene at the great high place of Gibeon, where Joab makes his stand.

themes: the right of sanctuary and its limits
the altar no shield for a murderer
a law even the heathen felt

schools.summary: Henry reads the execution as removing blood-guilt from David's house — blood not required from the murderer is required from the magistrate — so those who would have their houses safe must put iniquity far away.
Gill attends to the detail: Joab slain on the spot and buried in his own sepulchre, not by the altar, since the priestly Benaiah could not handle his burial.
Henry weighs the aggravations — Joab slew two better and more righteous men who meant him no wrong, and endangered David's own reputation — so the crime was the more heinous.
Gill reads the sentence as exact retribution, blood for blood, returned by God upon the murderer's own head.
Henry holds curse and blessing together — guilt entailed on Joab's seed, peace forever on David's — 'righteousness and peace kiss each other,' the peace traced to the Lord alone (Proverbs 25:5).
Gill: the blood returns on Joab's posterity as long as any remain, his death alone being no sufficient satisfaction.

themes: peace for ever from the Lord

schools.summary: Henry marks the private, criminal's burial yet no indignity to the body, and turns Joab's failed grasp on the altar into a call to the surer refuge of Christ's righteousness.
Clarke notes Joab, old and infirm, bleeds at last for Abner, Amasa, and Uriah — a servant of the state, but a murderer vengeance would not suffer to live.
Gill sets the deed at the great high place of Gibeon.

Henry reads the promotion of Benaiah and Zadok as the reward of fidelity and the fulfilment of the word raising a faithful priest for Eleazar's line (1 Samuel 2:35) — sacred offices may be disgraced but not destroyed.
Keil records both appointments; Ellicott notes no successor is named to Benaiah's former command over the body-guard.
Gill notes Benaiah advanced from captain of the bodyguard to general of the army.

Keil sets the geography of Bahurim and Kidron; Ellicott judges the confinement a reasonable precaution yet in some degree a snare — legally justifiable, perhaps politic, but not defensible on equity or generosity.
Henry calls it a fair trial of obedience on the easiest of terms, of which Shimei had no reason to complain.
Clarke reads it as leniency — the whole city for a prison, no hardship at all.
Gill notes Shimei summoned from Bahurim in Benjamin.

debates.question: Was Shimei's confinement a fair provision of life or a designed snare?

sides.position: Mercy — life granted on the easiest terms, a fair test of loyalty
A snare — reasonable on its surface but intended to reach an old grudge

themes: a fair test or a snare

schools.summary: Clarke reads the terms as prudent watchfulness over a seditious man; 'thy blood on thine own head' makes any breach Shimei's self-incurred guilt.
Keil notes Shimei vowed obedience on oath and kept it a considerable time.
Gill explains the Kidron is named because it lay on the road home to Bahurim, the crossing Shimei would most be tempted to make.

themes: thy blood on thine own head

schools.summary: Gill hears relief in Shimei's answer — expecting a traitor's death, he receives instead a comfortable exile in the delightful city of Jerusalem, very agreeable to him.
Keil stresses Shimei vowed obedience on oath and kept it for a considerable time, sealing the arrangement on both sides.
Keil supplies the circumstance of the slaves fleeing to Achish of Gath, David's old refuge; Ellicott notes the chronological puzzle and suggests the name Achish was hereditary in Gath's royal house.
Gill marks the three years Shimei had dwelt quietly in Jerusalem before the occasion arose.
Henry, through Bishop Hall, draws the moral: seeking his servants Shimei lost himself; men run out of God's bounds to hunt earthly things till their souls incur judgment.
Gill reads a double cause — Shimei's own passion and covetousness, and the providence of God leaving him to his lust that his old sin might find just punishment.
Clarke marvels that with his eyes wide open Shimei ran into the jaws of death.

themes: a life forfeited for a trifle

schools.summary: Henry marks the moment Solomon 'takes the forfeiture,' reading Shimei's breach as the expected transgression and a righteous providence bringing his old sins to judgment.
Gill supposes the report came by spies set to watch Shimei's motions, or by others who saw him go and return against the king's orders.
Henry frames the double contempt — of God's oath and the king's command — and reads Shimei's quiet evasion, presuming on ignorance or connivance, as an affront in the highest degree.
Gill names the offence as perjury — breaking an oath sworn in the Lord's presence and appealing to his name.
Keil notes the king turns from the broken oath to the deeper guilt of Shimei's old evil against David.
Keil hears the two counts joined — the oath of Jehovah and the king's commandment — before Solomon turns to the older crime; the question is rhetorical, admitting no defence.
Gill fixes on the sacred character of the broken oath, sworn in the Lord's presence, making Shimei guilty of perjury.

themes: the oath of the Lord and the commandment

schools.summary: Henry presses the appeal to conscience — Shimei's own heart is instead of a thousand witnesses; if the heart is privy, God is greater than the heart (1 John 3:20).
Keil: the evil his conscience must tell him is now returned by Jehovah, the death deserved for cursing the Lord's anointed (2 Samuel 16:5); Ellicott concedes the words refer back to the old offence.
Gill reads it as God's providence bringing an old sin to reckoning through a new one.
Henry sets the blessing against Shimei's impotent curses ('Let them curse, but bless thou') and lifts it to the church's comfort — Christ's throne is established and cannot be shaken.
Keil ties the blessing to the justice done — the throne is established because the king does justice.
Clarke states the principle Solomon acts on: while bad men go unpunished the nation cannot prosper, and justice was required for the throne's establishment.
Gill reads the blessing as a long, peaceable reign and large dominions despite every attempt against him.
Keil holds the punishment just — Shimei had his life in his own hand and broke his oath without excuse; the pleas in his favour have no force.
Clarke, with Kennicott, argues David's charge, rightly read with a carried-over negative, forbade Shimei's death — confirmed by Solomon confining rather than killing him at first.
Henry takes the plain reading and lifts the ending to Christ, King of peace yet of righteousness, whose final reckoning Solomon's justice foreshadows; the throne is a type of the Redeemer's kingdom.
Gill notes Shimei taken to the proper place of execution, the kingdom now established in Solomon's hand.

debates.question: Did David's dying charge command Shimei's death or, rightly read, forbid it?

sides.position: It commanded justice; Shimei rightly dies for breaking his own oath
With a carried-over negative it forbade shedding his blood; Solomon's own restraint confirms it
